nativeRange.habitat: Attached to hard substrate in shallow coastal reef zones and intertidal rocky shores; photic zone
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical and subtropical shallow reef and rocky intertidal zones

decorPreferences: rubble for holdfast attachment — do not bury in sand bed

avoidWith: heavy herbivorous grazers that may consume it before it establishes

imageCandidates.notes: Uploaded by 葉子 (yezi); 1024x768. Live golden-brown clump in a rocky tidepool, sharp, showing the thick serrated fronds and typical Sargassum ilicifolium coloration and open habit — best whole-plant view.
Uploaded by 葉子 (yezi); 1024x768. Sharp branch detail clearly showing the round air-filled pneumatocysts and toothed frond margins — strong diagnostic view.
Uploaded by 葉子 (yezi); 1024x768. Sharp macro of a single pneumatocyst (air bladder) on the frond axis; tight crop that cuts off the plant, kept as a diagnostic extra only.

summary: Sawtooth Kelp (Sargassum ilicifolium) is a brown macroalga from Indo-Pacific reef and rocky intertidal zones, recognized by its thick, serrated fronds and small air-filled bladders. It tends to grow slowly and develops a sparse rather than bushy habit, which is normal for the species, and generally adapts well to reef display tanks when anchored to rockwork.
careNotes:
Sawtooth Kelp belongs to the genus Sargassum (family Sargassaceae, order Fucales) and is distinguished by its thick, toothed or serrated leaf-like fronds and small pneumatocysts (air-filled bladders that help keep the fronds buoyant and upright). Coloration tends toward brownish-yellow or gold. The plant naturally develops a sparse, open habit rather than a dense bushy form — this is normal for the species and is not a sign of poor health.

Like other brown algae in the phylum Phaeophyta, Sawtooth Kelp must never be buried in the sand bed — if the base is submerged in substrate, it will melt. Instead, attach the plant by wedging it between rockwork, securing it with zip-tie or fishing line, or using cyanoacrylate (superglue) gel for frag-plug attachment. A display tank with stable, moderate flow and full-spectrum lighting is generally advised; actinic-only spectra are considered insufficient for brown macroalgae and are best avoided.

Water parameters should follow standard tropical reef targets: temperature around 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1,250–1,350 ppm, nitrate 5–20 ppm, and phosphate 0.02–0.1 ppm. Weekly dosing of iron, iodine, and other trace elements is generally recommended to support healthy macroalgae growth. Sargassum is widely considered palatable to herbivorous tangs and surgeonfish, so caution is warranted in reef tanks housing heavy grazers.

For genus-level context, see the related [pseudo-kelp] entry (Sargassum sp.). Sawtooth Kelp is a distinct species with the defining characteristic of sawtoothed frond margins, rather than a generic Sargassum trade specimen.
breedingNotes: Propagation in aquariums is generally achieved by fragmentation — detaching a branch tip and securing it to rockwork by wedging or with zip-tie or fishing line until the holdfast re-establishes. Sexual reproduction follows the alternation-of-generations lifecycle typical of the Sargassum genus but has not been reported as a practical propagation method in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros — Sawtooth Kelp
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Confirms species identification as Sargassum ilicifolium, sawtooth frond morphology, pneumatocysts, brownish-yellow coloration, very slow growth rate, sparse habit, and captive adaptability. No numeric water parameters on product page.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Macroalgae Care: The Basics
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source per playbook. Provides authoritative parameter targets: temp 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1,250–1,350 ppm, NO3 5–20 ppm, PO4 0.02–0.1 ppm. Explicitly states brown algae must not be buried in sand bed — base will melt. Recommends full-spectrum LED, moderate flow, and weekly iron/iodine micronutrient dosing for all macroalgae.
